title: Waveguides the Foundation of Silicon Photonics By dr. Moh Kolbehdari
gist: A brief technical primer arguing that waveguides—optical analogues of wires guiding photons through a high-index silicon core on silicon-on-insulator platforms—are the foundational layer of silicon photonics, with geometry choices (width, etch depth, bend radius) cascading through every downstream component from modulators to co-packaged optics for AI interconnects.

insights.claim: The waveguide is the foundational layer of silicon photonics—the optical equivalent of a wire—that must exist before any modulator, detector, ring resonator, splitter, WDM system, or co-packaged optics architecture can function, guiding photons through a high-index silicon core using oxide cladding on a silicon-on-insulator platform.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Waveguide yield and propagation loss are root-cause bottlenecks for every silicon photonics product; process control at waveguide level determines viability of CPO and AI optical interconnect scaling.
insights.quote: Before there is a modulator, detector, ring resonator, splitter, WDM system, optical engine, or CPO architecture, there must be a controlled path for light.
insights.locator: Waveguides are the foundation of silicon photonics

insights.claim: Small changes in waveguide geometry—width, silicon thickness, etch depth, bend radius, sidewall roughness, effective index, dispersion, polarization behavior, and fabrication tolerance—can alter the behavior of the entire photonic system, making waveguide design a system-level discipline rather than a passive routing task.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Manufacturing process variation at waveguide level is an underappreciated risk in CPO and AI optical interconnect scaling; foundries with tight waveguide process control have a structural moat.
insights.quote: Small changes in geometry can change the behavior of the entire photonic system.
insights.locator: waveguide design is not just drawing an optical line

insights.claim: Strip waveguides (fully etched, compact, high confinement) suit tight routing while rib waveguides (partially etched) are preferred when electrical access is needed—as in modulators and phase shifters—illustrating how waveguide design choices propagate into system architecture decisions.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Strip vs. rib tradeoffs constrain co-packaged optics (CPO) module design; vendors offering low-loss strip waveguides have a structural cost advantage in high-density AI optical interconnects.

insights.claim: For AI optical interconnects and co-packaged optics, the waveguide must interface cleanly with modulators, detectors, couplers, fiber attach, thermal control, calibration, packaging, and test—making the waveguide a full-system integration challenge, not just a passive optical routing element.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Full CPO system integration complexity is anchored at the waveguide layer; companies solving end-to-end waveguide-to-system integration own the value chain in AI optical interconnects.
insights.locator: For AI optical interconnects, CPO, and photonic integrated circuits
